commentBody: Is there some trick to completing the medium puzzle. I have all the squares filled in and correctly I think but not even any message saying I an almost there.
I found this one to be a decent, solid puzzle, but quite a bit easier than the Friday one.

As an American boarding a flight to Paris,
this one was mine. 

All mine!

Loved every minute.

I’m exhausted.
Need to sleep.
Excited. 

But I saw myself in the puzz and that’s all I need.

Beauty!

Bon soir!

I can brook Katie's TROUTS, and this was a pleasant solve, but I was right not to "fear" Saturday after the tougher than recently usual Friday. In the old days, we would say the two days' puzzles were swapped.

Saturday's midi - a real scorcher!

🥵

Katie describes her grid (a) having double diagonal symmetry, and says it “incidentally” (b) has the usual 180 degree rotational symmetry.
Exercise for the reader: prove that (a) implies (b), i.e. it’s not incidental.

Ha- we just needed some math, right? to balance out the toy poodles playing with pool noodles!

Yes! Yes! Yes! Thank you so much. I actually feel like I accomplished something and it took 30 minutes. God, I love that feeling. The NW and the SW had me. That was really fun. I’ll sleep like a baby.

When you see Katie Hoody on a byline you know you are in for a treat and this didn’t disappoint.
Like Steve L and Barry I solved it faster than yesterday but not sure it was “easier” and not sure that will be the consensus. It was just in my wheelhouse. Classic movie actresses (LORETTA)—check. Astronomy (LEONIDS)—check. Geek who still says “Up the WAZOO”—check. Piano player (LEDGERLINES)—check. Andrew Jackson trivia (kitchen cabinet CRONIES)—check. Particle physics (PION, TAU)—check check. Pokémon—OK, no, but I could at least guess it started with SNOR. And unfortunately my Gene Kelly/George Gershwin fetish didn’t help me with American in Paris, but still…
Tons of fun misdirections—MORN, SHOES, AZURE, GRATER, HERS, SCALP, PDA—who says short entries can’t be clever?
I had OODLES of fun with this one (or at least two OODLES)—thanks Katie!

I was TEETERing on the verge of having to sleep on this one and finish solving over my morning cup of OOLONGTEA,but I kept telling myself ITLLBEFINE, I SWEARONIT, the aha moment is YETTOCOME.  And sure enough before I was TOOFARGONE, everything clicked into place.  The NE plagued me greatly until KAMA, as in Kama Sutra, popped into my head. This was a good Saturday workout for me.
Anyone else rack their brain tonight about whether northern Michigan (upper peninsula—U.P.—youppi!) ever had a baseball team?
found it pretty challenging, good times

"You're shredding all this cheese for yourself?"
"No, it's for the grater gouda."

("Well, thank you dairy much!")
Loved this! Tough, chewy, fair, lots of groans, and kept me guessing until the end. Great puzzle.

One of two typically used when rowing…

I was thinking either oar, but it was LAT to be.

TROUTS is used without comment in the Canadian Encylopedia (https://thecanadianencyclopedia.ca/en/article/trout) as follows: "Trouts include the brown trout (Salmo trutta) and Atlantic salmon (S. Salar). Rainbow and cutthroat trouts are placed in the genus Oncorhynchus(O. mykiss and O. clarki respectively) along with Pacific salmon". And for the Yanks out there, Merriam-Webster says the plural is "trout also trouts" (https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/trout) So I conclude that as long as we're referring to multiple varieties, TROUTS (like fishes) doesn't really deserve any side-eye.

Didn't crack into this puzzle until 34A (ANOS). From there, correctly guessed PAISLEY and OOLONG TEA, and seemed to be off-and-running, having a reasonably quick time with the rest of the SW and SE.

But, then, it got tough for me, as I nibbled away at the NW and NW, laboriously working my way toward the middle.

Great puzzle. Thanks

I don't know about feelings, but this puzzle was hard.  There were some blank spaces that refused to budge and I got some help, but I still didn't get any congratulations.  At least the typo finally turned up without too much grief, and all the fills, when I finally figured them out, made perfect sense.  (Well, IRE was a little iffy, but even so, we ARE cool.)

Thank you, Katie Hoody.  Nice to see you haven't lost your touch.

I found it difficult…38 minutes slower than my average.  The NE corner and some middle squares (CRONIES) were falling just out of reach, until I lucked upon WAZOO.  All started falling into place then and the final solve proved most satisfactory!

Very nice Saturday!

userLocation: Spring Hill, just south of Armageddon
commentBody:
I suspect the days of cruciverbal metempsychosis and/or blissful barrages of misdirection/wordplays that knock your socks off are largely things of the past.  (Please let me be wrong!)  So I'll take a 40-minute Saturday skull buster any week.

48D twinged me nostalgically/basebally and 2D simultaneously tickled my rib and tensed me up for the non-'Murican cohort's inevitable backlash.

And because into every good grid a little rain must fall, I must direct a burn at INURN, which should never appear anywhere but at a mortuarian convention.

Thank you Ms. Hoody.
I thought 50A started with "p" for a bit. I guess I don't know Dickensons's work very well.

Signs of losing my edge.  I had the WATT part of 6A.  I also had the  second letter of I.

I spent a truly humbling amount of time thinking "there's no way a kitchen microwave is using GIGAWATTs.

Oh...right...KILOwatts.

Beginning to see why that old dream of a Nobel prize never really materialized, there, Francis.

While I enjoyed the clue, I recently learned that in fact brook trout are not TROUTS at all! They are a species of char!

On the other hand, today I discovered what a flathead trout is, so I'll call it even 🙂

Most fun I've had in a while. Not too much pop culture, a lot of general knowledge, so nothing seemed impossible. Interesting and clever cluing.

Kudos, Ms. Hoody!
Great puzzle! And Snorlax!
Katie Hoody is one of my top 5 favorite constructors. Her puzzles are always the perfect difficulty for me. This one took me twice as long as yesterday and I loved every minute.
The crossing of TOY POODLE, POOL NOODLE and ARE WE COOL was incredibly satisfying. As was the clue for TSA PRE. Thank you for an excellent Saturday puzzle.

Just realized my cat is a SNORLAX.

SWEAR ON IT there are many more Saturday grids YET TO COME from Katie Hoody.

❤️

Hardest puzzle in a long while, for me at least. Made it to the end at last when I flipped ORS to ERS ... I'm bingeing ER right now so I should've picked the right one first....  Satisfying to complete.
I have accepted that Saturday puzzles are hard for me but oh boy this one was up a notch. CLARITA KATIE, POR FAVOR!
Loved seeing all the OOs and SNORLAX. I can't be the only one who got stuck on "Hindu counterpart of Eros" because the capital-E suggested the god rather than the concept, right?

Crikey! I asked for a Saturday and really got a Saturday! Tough tough tough but so much more gratifying to solve as a result. 
For anyone at the NYT reading … this is what a Saturday is supposed to be like.

I think that settles the difficulty issue. Sometimes they do offer a particularly chewy one. It doesn’t mean that they haven’t evolved but this one was a Saturday to remember. 

TSAPRE for non USAians was a leap. 

I spent a long time in the top left. Started with CANOE went to Kayak/skull, experimented Salon instead of SCALP (gave the L for the brilliant LORETTA) and of course not knowing WAZOO SWIFT took a while to land. 

in the end it was solved by pure perseverance

Thanks to the setter and editors
I assume there is a contingent here confessing that when they finally got 6D from crosses, they went "Oh, yeah...the Sutra thing."
*TOY*POODLE? Pfft! https://imgur.com/a/lhXbssw
With both the TAU and its possible decay product the PION this puzzle was particle-ularly fun!

Some ponies and their cronies
Drank iced oolong by the pool
While toy poodles with foam noodles
Swiftly paddled a canoe.
The snorlax awoke to the smell of fresh trout
That (poof!) soon went in to his welcoming snout.
The sky was azure, sunshine out the wazoo
“Are we cool?” asked the ponies. But they already knew.
Trout Fishing in America https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=o2uV3WJhGbU A properly chewy Saturday puzzle. My last puzzle for a while, I'm off to the wilds of Jersey.

While not as challenging for me as yesterday's puzzle, this was still an enjoyable puzzle. [Emily Dickinson's early period] for MORN was excellent. I wish I believed 37 A, but I'm struggling with that. If you have some time today, check out an event near you: https://www.nokings.org/
One of those Saturdays that reminds you you’re not quite there yet — having near tripled the xwstats median solve time.

It’s plain to see that Katie spends time with every clue, seeking fresh angles.  I especially like how she zizzifies ordinary answers with out-of-the-ordinary clues, as she did with OWIES, MORN, PDA, HERS, GRATER and that terrific [Program for those trying to reduce screen time, familiarly?] for TSA PRE.

Zizzification continued with the pop in the box from never-seen-before answers and their never-seen-before clues.  Answer debuts aren’t guaranteed to be colorful, but Katie’s nine are today, including ARE WE COOL, MEDIA HYPE, SWEAR ON IT, YET TO COME, and TOO FAR GONE.

Not to mention non-debut answer loveliness (AZURE, POOF, DROLL, USURP, SAUNTERS, WAZOO, TEETER), and – ooh! – eight double O’s.

Total brain engagement and puzzjoy for me.  

I expected it, though, after experiencing your six previous puzzles, Katie, with their riddles galore, crackling cluing, pop, and beauty.  My heart did a little happy-leap when I saw your name atop the box, and it will next time too – thank you!

Hard enough for me. Couple of look ups giving me a few ILT moments. Looked to see if Emily Dickinson had a blue or grey period until I got enough cross letters for the "MORN" aha moment and smiled. I enjoyed typing Toy Poodle. I do miss my blind little red toy poodle, Rosie.
On Saturdays, I always enjoy looking at the grid design before diving in!
I thought of GRAPEVINE, as in “I heard it through…”  I wasn’t aware of the telegraph origins of this phrase. Hope the dits and dots created an equally catchy beat.

This is the first Saturday in a long time that I've come in above my average (if only by about a minute). A great mix of cluing and trivia and the occasional logical guess (SNORLAX sounded better than some alternatives). Great puzzle today.

LORETTA was the first gimme that shouted out to me. so I didn't expect the NW to be the last to fall. As a pen and paper solver, I don't write in answers until I'm almost 100% sure of them, so I struggled mightily. I had a faint inkling of what a columbine is, so I thought it was "inter" instead of INURN. I also immediately thought of "salon" for 1D, instead of SCALP, and for WAZOO, I've only heard it - and used it - as "up the..." Finally, it all came together, and I was thrilled that for two days in a row we had puzzles that were appropriately challenging.

Cool grid design as well as some fun entries and clues. I like LEONIDS, MEDIA HYPE, PAISLEYS, OOLONG TEA [Emily Dickinson’s early period] for MORN, and of course all the OOs. 

Katie Hoody is a good constructor, no trout about it.

A tougher than usual Saturday for me, with more than a couple of complete unknowns (SNORLAX POOLNOODLE). And.. had to look up some stuff to get through it. Probably shouldn't count it, but no big deal. And... puzzle find today - one of the most amazing ones I've ever encountered: A Sunday from February 1, 2015 by David Steinberg with the title: "This N' That." Some theme answers in that one: SUMMONSUBSTANCE COFFINWHEEZE FOREIGNTWENTY KRAKENPEEL FISSIONCHIPS FOREMANFUNCTION WARRENPEACE DRAGONDROP Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=2/1/2015&g=71&d=A ....

Caitlin thinks that the "grapevine" that produced HEARSAY was "probably" the first telegraph lines. Huh??? I'm pretty sure that the HEARSAY is the "gossip" that one hears through the "grapevine". Doesn't Caitlin know the wonderful "Heard It Through the Grapevine"? My favorite version, and today's earworm, is by Gladys Knight and the Pips. (I love me some Pips!) https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=WWvwP72FuVg But Marvin Gaye and Creedence Clearwater Revival did great with the song, as well. What's your favorite version?

Katie Hoody is fast becoming a crowd pleaser and personal favorite. This grid was full of fun and worthy of a Saturday challenge. Katie’s notes regarding grid design sent it over the top for me. As did SNORLAX.

One thing I like about long difficult wordplay answers is that once I get them I’m pretty confident they’re right. They’re not like proper nouns where any letter could be different than expected. Actor’s stage names are the worst. They have to be unique and are dreamt up by creative people. 

I really enjoyed today’s puzzle, but for less time than usual.
More puzzles at this challenge level, please!

A tough one for me, not knowing any Pokemon whatsoever or Wazoos, and can't imagine ever saying ARE WE COOL, unless referring to temperature. 

I still think of Paisley patterns as something on the shawls my grandmother wore. From actual Paisley.

Did anyone else notice that brOom stick has the same number of letters as POOL NOODLE.

I think the best faux jousting weapon is a broomstick coated with a POOL NOODLE.
And then there are the "pugil sticks" used in Marine training.  But no one (but a Marine, perhaps) would think that that weapon was "playful".

Woke up in the middle of the night. 
And decided to do a Saturday grid. 

I really enjoyed the puzzle. But maybe not the best time to solve. Seemed like a Saturday level of difficulty that l used to. But I had to think too much.  So many words I didn’t know. But always gratifying when you don’t have to flyspeck, upon entering that last cell. 

I hope I can fall asleep again.
Really unpleasant puzzle to solve. Cumbersome, illogical, pedantic. Saturday is supposed to be difficult, but this was just totally bereft of any joy or entertainment. A slog and a bore.

What a great puzzle! The top left bit had me putting in the wazoo, taking out the wazoo, putting in the wazoo, taking out the wazoo, over and over, until I finally saw what TSAPRE was.  It was ugly, that’s what it was to someone unfamiliar with it, and ironically it increased my screen time. But the puzzle overall made up for it. Talk about amped-up coverage!

Before I started the puzzle this morning, I read a text I'd received overnight. It read, in part:

"Your TSA PreCheck application has been approved. TSA has issued you a Known Traveler Number for immediate use..."

Even so, I had TSAPRE completely filled in from the crosses and still didn't understand it. I was sure I'd made a mistake somewhere.

Feels like the Grey Lady gat an updo!
Thanks, Katie, for a real treat!

Great clues, great fill.

Hope this crowd likes their grids al dente..
Somebody pass the cheese grater . . .

This one defeated me. 

I simply never realized until today that Liam's surname is NEESON. I thought it was NEESOM. For years now. And since SNORLAX could have been anything for me ... well, busted.

This leaves me in a bit of a bind. I really want to say that the puzzle was too easy for a Saturday. It was ... and yet it broke my (very short) streak. So perhaps is wasn't after all?

Enjoyable puzzle, and certainly well constructed. And for the rest of my days I'll remember it's NEESON, not NEESOM! Probably won't remember SNORLAX for long.

I don't pay much heed to "streaks", but having one broken by being off by one letter in the alphabet will certainly be memorable.

Quite a brain teaser! This was the classic "step away and finish with fresh eyes" puzzle for me. I was completely stumped by the NW corner last night but this morning realized "locks" probably meant hair and "blues" was probably about the color.

I love when letter combinations start to conjure words. 4D almost felt like a 7-letter Wordle game because I had xxxxxGN, leading to the "aha!" moment and arriving at FOREIGN.

I also love a solved puzzle reward when you throw a coin in the well and hope a letter is correct. I, like many others was stumped on TSAPRE (I still don't get the CRONIES cross?) but graciously that was the only square in my way in the end.

Katie Hoody is SWIFTly becoming one of my favorite constructors. This was a delight to solve, in spite of TROUTS. I was ridiculously proud of myself for remembering SNORLAX.

Favorite clue/answer combo: 20D, "Program for those looking to reduce screen time, familiarly?" Hilarious, unless you're stuck in one of those monster lines at the airports.

Jumping in here to say the Midi today was a mountain of fun!

It breaks most of the construction rules we know and love (I'm looking at you, 19 and 21D...), and rewards the solver with a brilliantly animated theme.

If you haven't tried the Midi, (and can access it on the Games app), give this one a try.  It'll blow your mind!
I love this puzzle. This difficulty level is just right - a tough nut to crack, but once you open it, the rest just flows in a way that feels so satisfying. Kudos to Katie Hoody - you rock!

Re: LORETTA
The Loretta in the clue has been the only one in a NYT XWP clue from here -- Sun Apr 25, 1948  99D	Miss Young, actress.	Willard N. Jordan	Farrar -- until 1980.
1980: first Swit
1983: first Lynn
2012: Beatles lyric
2021: Lynch (US AG)

Yet again, a testament to the power of a good nights rest…

Gave this puzzle a go (as is my wont) shortly before bedtime and trudged through a handful of clues. After a few minutes I _peacefully_ closed my iPad and fell asleep (I’ve had to learn how to do this).

Fast forward to _this_ morning, post coffee and chocolate, I cracked open the iPad, tore through the rest of the puzzle, got lucky with ADESTE, (member of the Hebraic persuasion over here) and heard that sweet, sweet melody.
I really got got with 36A. Fantastic brain teaser and fun to look back on.
Great puzzle. Took me almost two hours which I love for a Saturday. And like all great difficult puzzles the clues were hard but the answers were gettable.

I was so sure that the answer to "Where locks are set" would be SALON.  But I couldn't make it work with LORETTA, of which I was even more certain.  The only other Young in old Hollywood was Robert, and he was too short.

For a while I couldn't find anything to confirm LORETTA.  But once I thought of PONIES, which gave me an LP, which gave me SCALP instead of SALON for 1D, I was off to the races, so to speak.

I needed five out of five letters to get OWIES for "targets of some kisses".  Boy, was that a tough clue!  But a completely fair one.  In fact, the whole puzzle seemed comprised of clues that were both tough and very fair.  A constant game of wits with the constructor.  What fun!  And almost no "who knows and who cares?" trivia that I needed to concern myself with.  Oh, yes, there was SNORLAX, but I can forgive him or her or it because there was only one of him or her or it in the grid.

My kind of puzzle.  I had to put on my thinking cap throughout, but I didn't suffer at all.  A most enjoyable and engrossing themeless.
Really enjoyed this one. Had to take a break and come back with fresh eyes but in the end came around for me. TIL that trouts is considered plural of trout. Nice challenging Saturday

OoooKay!  All y'all who've been complaining about those puzzles that were too easy --(not that you were wrong, but...)  
 Well, you can just HUSH now.  
There are claw marks on the tablecloth from my scratching and clawing to find a toehold.
Of course I knew LORETTA Young at once, and EPCOT was a gimme...I even got OOLONG TEA (my fave.)  But MANDALAS for the psychedelic art, something lacking HOPE for being "Beyond repair" and PUNNY for the barely-funny....Woof!

A puzzle when one whips the LEONIDS and TROUTS into place instantly should not keep me busy wearing down the eraser on my pen!  ADESTE fideles, indeed.  

I am hereby protesting the weensy skinny Comment box that won't expand as I type.  One more inconvenience for the Solvers and Commentariat?  

Other than that, Ms Katie Hoody, I have this to say:  WOW!  Talent out the WAZOO.  (I had that and took it out... tricky!)
Tough one. Upper left corner had me stuck till the end.
For 6 across I immediately filled in KLYSTRON. It worked with 6 down, but I quickly saw that it was not going to work with any other crosses.
The NW corner was a mystery until I got that second “o”. Great puzzle.

This was a delight! Thank you, Katie. Seemingly impenetrable at first, but little by little, it all came together. A perfect Saturday morning. 

I thought I started off with a bang with SALON at 1D. But the later correction to SCALP (thanks to LORETTA) was even better. I loved the crossing of TOYPOODLES with POOLNOODLES. Amazed that I pulled SNORLAX out of the deep recesses of my memory. (His buddy LORAX has appeared in other puzzles twice this past week!) 

I loved the whole (text?) conversation in AREWECOOL? TOOFARGONE? ITLLBEFINE. SWEARONIT? YETTOCOME. All debut entries! 

Yesterday, I was happy to be among XW Stats’ 50% who solved much slower (>20%) than their [recent] Friday average (glad to have the challenge). Today, it says 14% solved much faster (>20%) than their Saturday average. I was 31% faster, but I didn’t consider it much easier, it just happened to be right up my alley in several subjects. Loved every minute of it!
Crunchy!

I was going to complain about “trouts,” but since the constructor already asked forgiveness, let me recommend James Prosek’s book “Trout,” with his lovely illustrations. For me the trout, and the art of fly fishing, are signposts of the stark differences between now and then, when streams ran clean, kids searched for crawdads under rocks, and a person could fish in solitude without first flying to a remote destination and hiring a guide.
AREWECOOL -> TOYPOODLE -> POOLNOODLE was great!
Excellent!

I loved this puzzle. Yet it left me with a bittersweet taste - as often happens after completing a great puzzle. Kind of like waking up in the morning after a beautiful dream - just to realize it was only a dream. 
Thanks Katie
We are definitely cool! Brava!
wow! a really delicious combo of trivia (thank goodness for SNORLAX, the first entry today i could feel confident about...) and conversationalisms. really tough, and a real treat!

Anyone else here work at sea? 

Brown TROUTS has a completely different connotation at sea...

TROUTS could have been clued as [Michael the Angel, and others] because every single person here loves baseball clues.

I struggled with and enjoyed this puzzle which I(correctly) found to be more difficult than yesterday's.

There was a musical entitled "Bloody Bloody Andrew Jackson" about the president and his CRONIES. It was funny, sometimes in a juvenile way, with some really great songs.

While it was controversial for what some thought was an overly generous portrayal of him, I thought it satirically yet accurately showed Jackson for the belligerent, prejudiced, dishonest populist(he knew about popular) he was.

Sorry for the tangent that has absolutely no relevance to anything or anyone in the past, present or future.
ARE WE COOL? Yes we are. Oh, but that NW corner! I knew a columbarium was some part of a church, so why not a place to TITHE? Well, I thought it was pretty clever. Apparently not.

What a delightful and difficult puzzle! I really love having to get to the third or fourth meaning of a clue word, along with an unusual sense of an entry word, to finish the solve. “Hurried” for SWIFT was the only dubious one IMO.

Regarding TROUTS, I don't think any apology is needed. Any ichthyologist will tell you that, while "fish" is the plural when referring to multiple individual animals, "fishes" is the plural when referring to multiple *types* of animal. So by extension, surely the same is true for "trout" vs. TROUTS!
A perfect Saturday puzzle! Loved the noodle-eating poodle!
What a satisfyingly difficult puzzle! Great job. TSAPRE had me sweating as I unearthed each new letter and it seemed to make less and less sense!
This took me forever!! But what an excellent, Saturday-worthy puzzle!

I typically don’t comment on puzzles, since my experience with them waxes and wanes because I tend to fall asleep working them, or I get distracted, so my thinking isn’t always clear and quick and my solve times are erratic. That said, I feel a strong desire to call out probably my favorite answer I’ve ever encountered in my now 2+ years of solving: LEONIDS just delighted me!

I am often staring at the sky in wonder, picking out constellations or taking a moment to be dazzled by the moon. I’ve done this since I was a kid, when I learned how easily I could find the Big Dipper. I have witnessed more than a few meteors streak across my view while driving home at night or standing in my backyard after midnight. I managed to just catch what I’m pretty sure was a fireball meteor last week! 

Each time I’ve seen one, I’m left awed by the vast unknown expanse before me, and so deeply connected to my humanity. It’s as if I can envision being alive thousands of years ago, seeing the gods in the stars, while at the same time I feel myself connected to those YET TO COME, knowing they will certainly one day experience the very same beauty. It’s simultaneously a super grounding and weightless feeling. 

Thank you to constructor Katie Hoody for inspiring this reflection in me today! I hope all of you get a chance to look up at the stars once in a while. The Lyrids will be at peak visibility in the US at the end of April!
Great Saturday puzzle!

From the archives: In the Sunday 23rd June 2002 puzzle the clue “Crumb” solves to ORT 

Can someone explain it to me please 

By the way if you want a super puzzle try it.

Playing through Pokemon Fire Red over the past week and I was absolutely delighted to find a reference in today’s puzzle.  Easy layup for me but probably impossible for someone unfamiliar with the series
Stretched myself well past my average, and loved it! My best dog ever was a toy poodle, Thunder, long story short he rescued me. Cheers to you, Thunder.
TROUTS notwithstanding, this was an absolute joy of a puzzle. I was scratching my head over so many clues I'm practically bald, a sign of a really good puzzle. Thanks Katie!

Special shout-out to the misdirect at 36A where of course I had OAR as a gimme.  Messed up 12D because with all its other letter O's I missed the extra one.
  Anyone else have AREWEGOOD and then ITLLBEGOOD and think something was going on here? Oh but Saturday's don't have themes (what never?) so eventually I fixed them both.
very COOL GOOD FINE NEATO puzzle.

I love Katie’s puzzles! This one was very fun. Brought many smiles to my face, not only for the clever misdirects but also for the memories of my kids when they were young and into Pokemon and pool noodle battles. I particularly like the combo of AREWECOOL, ITLLBEFINE, and SWEARONIT. Thanks, Katie—I needed that.
Yes, a hard Friday followed by a challenging Saturday! Are the editors listening?? Thank you, great solve, hoping for a fun Sunday to round out the weekend!
I was working at 44D (Spaceship Earth) when Mission: Space opened. Crazy that it was so long ago.
We are so back

Not sure where to post TYs for the Midi, so…
Thank you!  Was fun
Great puzzle! If only KILOWATT had been KILOWATTREL to go along with SNORLAX!

"Pool noodle"? What's that? Why not the proper Nerf noodle? 

I would not have guessed "pool noodle" in a zillion years...

Only just seen OWIES - what on earth? I looked it up and found it is a real word, and similar to 'boo-boo' - a WHAT? My knowledge of US English expands. I can't think of a UK equivalent, apart from bruise, scrape, cut etc.
I thought 'locks' referred to the locks on canals ....
Clunk? 🙄

I thought this was going to take me an OOHLONG time to solve, but it was actually significantly faster than my Friday puzzle, which was well over my average. Today's was well under my average. Just goes to show! I don't know what it goes to show, but I'm sure it goes to show something...  

LAT instead of ore was an excellent reminder to not rely too heavily on crosswordese. Good one!  Enjoyed the long entries and the tricky little TSAPRE. All around enjoyable!

And now I'm off to ride some ponies with my cronies, while trying to remember the rest of the words to Adeste Fidelius, which has at least for the moment replaced Lemon Pound Cake for the song playing on repeat in my head.

Lemon pound cake!

Breaking my first ever 7+ day gold streak (12 days!!!!) on this but I'm not upset at all:) I love when a clue that originally trips me up ends up making another appearance (as they often do) or becomes randomly relevant in an IRL conversation. "OMG I learned this from the crossword!" feels so good to say, especially around my friends that I've been BEGGING to try the crossword.

Alright ... going to use Check Puzzle and finally say goodbye to gold ... remember meeee *fades to dust*
Fischer-Dieskau and Moore serve up a little bit of perfection, ledger-lines and all: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=wwmAbav2MZE&list=RDwwmAbav2MZE&start_radio=1

Bravo— a very satisfying Saturday puzzle. Was a buster to my average time, but that is a good thing. Some nice clever variations on clues. 
As a gimmick have you thought of an AI xword?
Really, really tough & really, really fun! Perfect Saturday puzzle.

Really good, Katie Hood! And I see they have figured out not to make it too easy on the bottom, but my tough places were in the Northwest. It all seems so simple now but “Blues” made me think either music or denim and didn’t get SCALP or WAZOO until I checked Puzzle after an hour and five minutes and then it all fell into place. The column didn’t seem to think those were tricky clues :)
But whew it’s like we have our Saturday back.
Embarrassed at how long it took me to figure out it's "NEESON" not "NEESOM"

Katie Hood, you are forgiven for TROUTS in exchange for this fun stumper of a puzzle. TROUTS was one of the few answers I got on the first pass, though yes, with a side eye.  
Looking forward to your next puzzle!
So much more fun than yesterday's puzzle! I really enjoyed it
proper Saturday keep it this way - those of us who stayed the course all these years deserve challenges thus-sat.  grazie mille'

I got "trouts" and don't really have a problem with it, but I think I would have phrased the question, "Flathead and brook are varieties," rather than use the word "these." That way I think the plural is more associated with varieties rather than a plural reference to the fish generally. 
Maybe I'm touched to think this quibble makes a difference but somehow it makes sense to me. 
Very tough but good puzzle.

Great puzzle, although I’ll lodge my usual complaint against the inclusion of baby words. TROUTS I justified by comparing the pluralization to how  “people” can become “peoples.” 

Like Caitlin, I got the grapevine clue before I *got* it (and thought myself clever for filling in HOT GOSS). 

Overall, a really fun solve. Thanks, Ms. Hoody & eds. We are definitely cool, and I look forward to what is YET TO COME!

The northeast corner nearly defeated my 150-something streak, but I made it through! And for the most part everything felt like "good" difficulty, the kind where you look back on clues and say "oh of course" or "TIL" rather than "ugh" or "I guess". Great a Saturday puzzle!

Our constructor's surname led me to some searches that resulted in some interesting puzzles. Specifically, I was looking for answers containing HOOD. Here's one puzzle: A Tuesday from February 12, 2013 by Dan Schoenholz. Four theme answers, straightforwardly clued. Just think about it. BOYZNTHEHOOD ELEPHANTTRUNK FORTYYARDDASH FREDDYFENDER Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=2/12/2013&g=20&d=A I'll put the others in a reply. ...

Overall a nice, crunchy Saturday, reminiscent of the days of yore, but I gotta say, there are few things I dislike guessing at more in crosswords puzzles than Pokemon names. Even when I (eventually) get them right, they look so wrong and I can never be sure :/

Very challenging for me, but that is expected of a Saturday puzzle. I really enjoyed working on it. I recognized the constrictor's name and knew what I'd be up against. One question, though:
4D - As the article "an" is used, "American" is a noun and not an adjective. So, wouldn't the answer be the noun "foreigner"? [The nuns taught me grammar in French, English, Spanish and Latin.]

Never in my 5 months of doing this thing has there been a more useless part of a clue than (Offenbach opera). I’d love to know who looked at that clue and thought that it would make it easier to know that Contes d’Hoffman needed extra qualification. Is there another Contes d’Hoffman out there that is not an Offenbach opera and starts with a different word? If there is I will happily stand corrected and apologise to the author or editor that put it in.
(This is tongue in cheek before anyone goes off on me).

Apart from that I knew Snorlax and not much else, started Check Puzzle after about 10 minutes on this one, I’d often get to an hour before I do that. Slightly mollified to find out it was actually somewhat hard.
Today’s puzzle was super satisfying! Some delightfully clever clues. Thank you 👏

Today's poem made from words found in today's puzzle

a/ the worry particle of feelings
moves hurried up the cabinet
no need to eat no need sleep
feet! feet!
d/ it’s the trying the happening the enragement
of being
and beyond the psychedelic light
where are the silent a/ ponies

This was a great solid Saturday puzzle and judging by a lot of the comments I think many would agree.  Monday esoteric terms.  Very smart clues.  And how can one not appreciate the Pokemon reference!!  Excellent puzzle, Katie!

And... the comments regarding 'foreign' below led me in a roundabout way to another quite unusual puzzle. A Sunday from February 1, 2015 by David Steinberg with the title: "This 'N That." Clue and answer that led me there: "Venti, vingt or zwanzig?" FOREIGNTWENTY Another sample clue and answer: "Intel products used at a nuclear facility?" FISSIONCHIPS And some other theme answers: SUMMONSUBSTANCE HERONMAKEUP COFFINWHEEZE DRAGONDROP KRAKENPEEL FOREMANFUNCTION WARRENPEACE Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=2/1/2015&g=71&d=A ....
Earned a snail by :34.  Good workout.

I braced myself when I saw Katie Hoody's name (and I mean that in the BEST way possible). I grabbed a snack, a cozy blanket, and hunkered down for what I knew would be a tough but satisfying Saturday solve, and I was not disappointed! SO many glorious aha moments throughout, and zero "hmm ....I...guess??" moments. (Though I did have to consult my kids for SNORLAX) 
Hope to see another one from you soon, Katie!

It did not disappoint! After yesterday's Saturday level, I was all set to remain hungry for brain food after today's, but nay. I am sated. I had large blank areas for the longest time, got called away and when I came back, still nothing. But then--something. And  then--everything. Those opaque clues became paque. 

Which reminds me. My youngest brother used to invent words. You could purchase your own personal one for a nickel. I asked him what mine meant and he said the nickel only purchased the word, not the definition. I think he was 7 at the time.

Challenging and fun. I certainly didn't finish it in one go, but I had many runs at it--in between other tasks (and fun things like going to our local No Kings rally with my daughter)--I filled in a bit more. Eventually I used a few of Caitlin's Tricky Clues fills. So it wasn't a fast solve but a fun one.Thanks to Katie and the whole team.

This took me forever! “It’ll be gone,” “good as gone,” did not know ledger line or Leonids, “DOB” for “Gen.” Probably my worst Saturday time in 20 years.

In my defense, we have the whole fam down at a friend’s beach house on the Oregon coast and it is utterly cloudless, with temps in the 60s, so I was pretty distracted.

Beach fire at sunset tonite! And I made crab cakes yesterday from the four Dungeness crabs I bought off the old guys on the bridge who were hauling them up from their pots. Wrap them up in foil and enjoy them warmed up with a cold beer soon. Life is good. And the dog is really, really tired.
CRONIES was timely.

I really didn't think I was going to make it through the puzzles this weekend, but I sat down to fill in just a little bit on this one so I could try to finish it tomorrow and ended up doing the whole thing in one (long) sitting. I don't think this was the most "fun" puzzle for me but it was certainly engrossing. Had to peek at the tricky clues but that's okay. One day away from my first week long streak!🤞🏻
I hate that this column opens in the game app instead of the news app now. Please change this back!

Check out the new(ish) Broadway musical, Operation Mincemeat, for some history, brilliant wordplay, and a song featuring a trout!

We saw it in London and met the lovely cast/creators of the show, so then we had to come see them in New York.
Some tricky, fun clues in this one. Got stuck and had to come back Sunday morning with fresh eyes to finish.
@Geoff Offermann did you do the clue in red, 28 across? I almost missed that one.
@Bluebonnet  Ahhhh thanks.
@Steve L significantly harder for me. tough to pin down puzzle difficulty
@Steve L I breezed through yesterday, whereas I found today's a worthy adversary. Funny how that works.

@Steve L

This one was 50% over average and I enjoyed every minute.

@Steve L

Funny, today took me almost twice as long as yesterday — 30% slower than average today vs 9% faster for Friday’s.

@Steve it’s funny. Today was close to my record Saturday time, but I couldn’t finish yesterday without multiple lookups, which makes it the most difficult puzzle of at least the past few months for me.

One of my favorite things about crosswords is how every individual’s unique life experiences makes each puzzle unique to them. Your gimme might be completely unknown to me, and that impacts the rest of the puzzle. I try not to get too hung up about whether this puzzle might have fit better on a Friday, but whether the experience was enjoyable. I found today’s to be a great puzzle by that measure.

@CCNY My first thought for that one was INEXILE. Wonder what that says about my state of mind? Envying your April In Paris. Here is a very mellow remastered audio rendition by young Ella Fitzgerald and Louis Armstrong from 1932. https://tinyurl.com/3pbrksjy

@Al  in Pittsburgh. One tiny correction (and not your mistake, but the way it's presented on the video you linked to). The song itself dates to 1932. But the version Ella & Louis recorded together is from the 1950s. I don't know the exact year, and I'm too lazy to look it up right now.

@Barry Ancona

@SP

The first xwstats.com assessment was Very Hard, but that was based on a sample size (I assume) of three (33% vs. 67%; I guess it could have been six, nine, etc.).  It has since moved to Hard, which is, I suspect, where it will, and should, remain.
And as of this morning, the xwstats Saturday median solve time is faster than the Friday median solve time.
@Barry Ancona Barely? I enjoyed both of them and Friday was about 31 and today was about 30. I wish the Friday and Saturday puzzles were like these two every week.

I point this out not to be persnickety but because this one was tough for me because I've only ever heard it your way.

Jake,
I enjoyed both puzzles too, and I'm glad they were both harder than what we've seen recently, but the median solve time for today's puzzle is still a minute faster than yesterday's, and your personal results were the same.

@Grumpy. As with fish and fishes

@Grumpy Excellent citation! I mentioned this elsewhere, but my only actual qualm is that brook "trout", despite their name, are actually char, not trout. Same with their cousin, the lake trout. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Brook_trout https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lake_trout A bit of obscure fishy trivia for a Saturday.

@Grumpy "Trouts" (as in, "varieties of trout") grates on me far less than "softwares", which results from "software" losing its status as a mass noun. Does one run "a software" on "a hardware"?

I shouldn’t complain, though, because I didn’t mind when the same thing happened to "email".
'Trout' is not a term of scientific/ichthyoligic classification. Some 'trout(s)' are related to char(s), others to salmon(s).  They're still trouts if people call them that.

Ed,
Email as a message system never has a plural.
Email as a message sent through said system often does.

@Ben
Actually, there is no such thing as TROUTS. The plural of “trout” is “trout.”

Mark,
Actually...

@Mark Smith 

Indeed! 

I think this was meant as one of those grammatically vague "plural of plurals". I'm not defending it exactly, but in my head it's similar to the way you would say person > people for one or many individuals, but might refer to multiple larger ethnic groups gathered in one area as "peoples" (All the peoples of the earth were there).

In either case, imagine my surprise at learning their true identity after spending 30+ years catching brook trout!
A trout is whatever people call a trout. It's not a scientific taxon.

@Captain Quahog - and for those trying to suss out the answer etymologically, the "columb-" in "columbarium" is from the Latin "columba," meaning dove or pigeon. Seems a bit non-funerary, but the rows of niches in which the ashes are placed resemble the nesting boxes in a dovecote.

@Grant
Some churches do have a columbarium--especially those with little to no space remaining in a graveyard--but these may be associated with a funeral  home, or a city, state, or national 'place of rest.'  Our parents left their bodies to medical research/education; eventually their mingled ashes were placed i(by our elder sister) n a columbarium in the Decatur, GA, graveyard in a ceremony where students from the medical school at Emory spoke of the significance of this gift.  My sister felt it was very meaningful.

@Tali

By the way, doves are known as "mourning doves."  I believe this is due to their coo or cry which sounds like a person in mourning.
"Hurried" here is an adjective, not a (past tense) verb. It's fine.

@Barry Ancona Yes, and the adjective "swift" means speed with implied ease, while the adjective "hurried" means speed with implied force or anxiety. The intention and nuance are different, and they do not mean the same thing.

@Jacad

They definitely don’t mean the same thing (most words don’t!), but if something is done in a hurried fashion, it’s likely you could also say it was done swiftly.

Jacad,
Crossword clues are hints; they need not be, and late in the week rarely are, definitions or synonyms.
A bit of a spoiler for the unwary who look at the photo before finishing the puzzle?

@Anthony Haha, I love the way you described that because that’s exactly how it felt trying to solve that one until I finally got the P from the cross. “Is there some sort of social media detox camp program I’ve never heard of? Are we talking about Apple Screen Time?” 

It’s like when RANDB for “R&B” or AANDM for A&M show up in a puzzle and I question my ability to understand English!

@Ali J

What a terrific post!  Exactly the kind that delights me so about this forum.

There is a powerful comraderly with our long lost forefathers when staring at the night sky.  We are looking at very much the same thing they looked at.

Though not quite.   The Greeks never mentioned Polaris as being the "North Star".  That's because at the time it wasn't.  It was several degrees from due north, and so it wasn't particularly different from the others.

Also fascinating is the Big Dipper, part of Ursa Major, the Big Bear.  Both the ancient Greeks and North American Indian tribes identified *those same stars* as a Great Bear.  This would have been centuries before there was any contact between the two societies.

A few years ago I indulged myself in my dotage, and bought a really nice amateur telescope (two, actually).  While I was waiting for it to get off backorder, I watched "Our Night Sky" by Dr. Edward Murphy through the Great Courses.  It was purely a naked eye exploration of the constellations, and was truly wonderful.

Thanks you so much for a terrific post.  Don't be a stranger.
@Francis What I wouldn’t give to have seen Carl Sagan speak!! He’s definitely a hero for me too.
